UX and Product Design Case Study

Redesigning the ActivProperties Platform

A real estate CRM serving two very different people: an admin who needs to run the whole business from one screen, and an agent who just wants to focus on closing deals. I redesigned the entire platform so both of them could finally do that.

Solo Product Designer
3 to 4 Weeks
Real Estate SaaS
Admins and Agents
In Development
Figma
Overview

The Product and the Problem

ActivProperties is a real estate CRM built for agencies operating in the UAE property market. Two very different types of people use it every day. Admins who are responsible for the entire business, and Agents who spend their day talking to leads and trying to close deals.

The platform had everything it needed functionally. What it lacked was clarity. As the agency grew, the cracks started showing. Admins had to hunt for the numbers that should have been right in front of them. Agents were drowning in information that had nothing to do with their work. And when the business tried to demo the product to potential clients, they were embarrassed by how it looked.

My job was to redesign the whole thing so it worked properly for both users and looked like a product worth buying.

End to end UX and UI
Stakeholders and Dev
Admins and Agents
50 plus screens
In Development
1
Discovery

Stakeholder sessions and UI audit

2
Research

Phase 1 — Discovery

Listening Before Designing

Before I touched Figma, I spent time understanding the platform through stakeholder conversations and a thorough audit of the existing interface. No formal user interviews were in scope, but the combination of stakeholder feedback and firsthand analysis gave me a clear picture of what was actually broken.

Direct from a stakeholder

"Admins have been using this for months and still struggle to find things. New team members take forever to get started. And when we try to demo it to potential clients, it just does not look like a product they would pay for."

What admins were dealing with
  • No summary view of the business. Pipeline numbers were buried inside menus
  • Checking the status of a lead or deal meant opening multiple screens
  • Everything was weighted equally. Nothing told you what needed attention
  • Agent performance required manually pulling reports
  • The interface looked internal and unfinished, not something you could sell
What agents were dealing with
  • The platform showed admin level data that had nothing to do with their job
  • No personal view of their own leads, pipeline, or earnings
  • Finding the right property for a lead was entirely manual
  • The deal submission process had no clear stages or guidance
  • Activity history was disconnected from the lead profiles it belonged to
The real problem

The platform was built for features, not for people. It gave everyone the same dense interface regardless of their role. Admins felt overwhelmed. Agents felt lost. The redesign needed to start from scratch with a simple principle: show each person only what they need to do their job well.

Phase 2 — Research

Learning from the Market Leaders

Without formal user research, I turned to competitive analysis. I studied Bayut, Dubizzle, and Property Finder specifically looking at how they handled the UX challenges I was trying to solve. These are the platforms that UAE real estate professionals use every day, so they shaped what users already expect.

Feature / Pattern
Bayut
Dubizzle
Property Finder
KPI first dashboard
✓ Strong
~ Basic
✓ Strong
Role based navigation
✓ Yes
✗ No
✓ Yes
Card based property layout
✓ Yes
✓ Yes
✓ Yes
Visual lead stage tracking
~ Partial
✗ Weak
✓ Clear
Agent performance dashboard
✓ Yes
✗ No
✓ Yes
Smart lead to property matching
~ Partial
✗ No
✓ Yes
Avoids table only views
✓ Yes
~ Mixed
✓ Yes
What I took away

The best platforms in this market all shared the same three things: role specific views, KPI first dashboards, and card based layouts for properties. These were not differentiators. They were the floor. ActivProperties needed to reach this baseline before it could compete, let alone impress a client in a demo.

Phase 3 — User Personas

The Two People This Product Serves

Everything in this redesign flows from understanding these two people properly. They use the same platform but they have completely different goals, different daily routines, and different definitions of what a good day looks like. Getting both of them right in a single product was the central design challenge.

Omar Al Rashidi
Operations Admin, 38, Dubai Marina
Admin User

Omar is accountable for everything. Properties, leads, deals, agents, and reporting all land on his desk. He logs in every morning hoping to understand the full state of the business quickly, then spend the rest of the day making decisions and unblocking his team. What he gets instead is a maze of menus and no clear starting point.

8+yrs experience
12agents managed
Dailyplatform use
What he needs from the platform
  • Understand business health at a glance within the first two minutes
  • Review and approve deals without chasing agents for context
  • See where every lead stands without opening each one
  • Track which agents are performing and which need attention
  • Show the platform to new clients without feeling embarrassed
What made him frustrated
  • KPIs hidden inside menus, so every morning started with five clicks before seeing any numbers
  • No single place to see agent performance without exporting to a spreadsheet
  • A UI that felt too rough and unfinished to present to enterprise clients
"I should open this platform and know exactly where things stand in under a minute. Right now that takes me half a morning."
Sara Malik
Real Estate Agent, 27, Abu Dhabi
Agent User

Sara's whole day is about momentum. She picks up new leads, calls them, figures out what they need, finds the right properties, takes them on viewings, and pushes toward closing. Every minute she spends navigating a confusing interface is a minute she is not talking to a lead.

3yrs experience
15+active leads
Dailyplatform use
What she needs from the platform
  • See her assigned leads the moment she logs in, nothing else
  • Match leads to the right properties quickly with smart suggestions
  • Track exactly where each lead is in the pipeline without guessing
  • Submit deals and see their approval status without asking admin
  • See her own commission progress and monthly performance
What made her frustrated
  • The platform showed her admin data that had nothing to do with her daily tasks
  • Property matching required manually browsing listings with no smart filtering
  • After submitting a deal she had no idea where it was in the approval process
"I want to see my leads, find them the right place, and close. Everything else just gets in my way."
Phase 3 — Empathy Mapping

Thinking Through Their Experience

Personas tell you who someone is. Empathy maps help you think through what it actually feels like to be that person using your product. I mapped both users across what they think, feel, say, and do inside the platform, along with what causes them pain and what would genuinely make their day easier.

Omar's experience as admin

Carrying the whole operation on his shoulders every day.

Thinks
  • Are my agents following up with their leads today?
  • Which deals have been sitting without movement?
  • How is this month tracking compared to last quarter?
  • Will this product hold up when I demo it to that client on Friday?
Feels
  • Overwhelmed when the dashboard gives him no useful summary
  • Anxious when he cannot tell what actually needs his attention right now
  • Frustrated when he has to chase agents for updates instead of reading them
  • Embarrassed when the platform looks unpolished in front of a potential client
Says
  • "Why do I have to click five times just to check deal status?"
  • "I need to see what is overdue the moment I open this thing"
  • "This does not look like a product I can charge someone for"
Does
  • Opens three or four tabs side by side to check lead and deal status together
  • Messages agents directly because it is faster than using the platform
  • Exports data into a personal spreadsheet to build the reports he actually needs
Pains
  • Cannot read the business without doing real work first
  • Agent review requires manual report generation every time
  • Deal approval flow is slow because context is scattered across screens
Gains
  • A dashboard that tells the full story in under 60 seconds
  • Deal review that is fast because all context is already there
  • A product that feels worth demoing to enterprise clients

Sara's experience as agent

Running on momentum, easily derailed by anything that slows her down.

Thinks
  • Which of my leads has not heard from me in too long?
  • What properties should I be showing this person?
  • Did my deal get approved yet or is it still sitting with admin?
  • Am I going to hit my commission target this month?
Feels
  • Distracted by admin level data covering most of the screen
  • Unsure about where her submitted deals are in the process
  • Slowed down every time she has to manually browse for property matches
  • Motivated when she can actually see her own progress and earnings
Says
  • "I just need to see my leads, not everyone else's"
  • "Finding the right property takes way longer than it should"
  • "I submitted that deal three days ago and have no idea what happened to it"
Does
  • Keeps her own spreadsheet to track what is happening with each active lead
  • Texts admin directly asking for deal status updates
  • Manually scrolls through property listings instead of using match filtering
Pains
  • Platform was not designed around how she actually works
  • Zero visibility into her own commission or monthly performance inside the tool
  • Deal flow has no clear stages so she never knows what step comes next
Gains
  • A clean focused view of just her leads and listings
  • Property match scores that tell her what to show without manual searching
  • A deal tracker that shows exactly where things are at every stage
Phase 4 — User Flows

Mapping How Each Person Moves Through the Product

Before deciding on layouts or navigation, I mapped the two most important journeys in the product. The admin flow covers how Omar goes from logging in to reviewing a deal and checking on his agents. The agent flow covers how Sara goes from picking up a new lead all the way to submitting a deal. These flows were built directly from the BRD logic and shaped every structural decision that came after.

Admin Flow Omar
Login
Dashboard
KPI cards, listing performance, and channel data
Leads Module
All leads across the agency with stage visibility
Lead Profile
Contact info, property interest, activity log
Deals Module
Filter by pending admin review
Deal Review
Commission details, documents, key parties
Approve or request changes?
Approve, goes to finance
Request change, back to agent
User Management
Agent performance, deals, and conversion
Session complete
Agent Flow Sara
Login
My Leads
Only her assigned leads, sorted by last activity
Lead Profile
Budget, purpose, property interest, and priority
Property Matches Tab
Smart matched listings with percentage scores
Shortlist Property
Notifies seller agent, added to shortlisted tab
Site Visit Logged
Captured in activity log with notes
Initiate Deal
Proposed amount and commission filled in
Seller agent approves?
Approved, fill buyer details
Rejected or counter offer
Submit Deal to Admin
Documents, key parties, commission all filled
Pending admin review
What mapping these flows revealed

The admin and agent journeys share almost no overlap. They needed completely separate mental models, not the same screen with slightly different data. The deal flow was also far more complex than it appeared on the surface, touching multiple people across multiple stages before anything is finalised. This became the most carefully designed part of the entire product.

Phase 5 — Information Architecture

Rebuilding the Navigation from Scratch

The old navigation was organised around features. Every module had the same weight, the same visual treatment, and the same level of visibility regardless of whether it was something users touched every day or once a month. I rebuilt it around tasks and roles instead.

Everyone sees the same navigation regardless of their role
→
Role based navigation so each user sees only what belongs to their job
No clear starting point. Every module competes for attention equally
→
Dashboard as the entry point every session, with KPIs always front and centre
Lead information scattered across three or four disconnected screens
→
One unified lead profile with property matches, activity, and discussion in a single place
Deal submission has no visible stages and no guidance on what comes next
→
Deals module with explicit stage progression and a clear review flow for every role
Agent performance completely invisible without manually generating reports
→
Dedicated agent profile pages with performance charts and deal history built in
Phase 5 — Design System

The Foundation Everything Else Was Built On

Before I designed a single screen, I built the visual system. This is the part that most people skip or treat as an afterthought, but it is what makes a product feel like one coherent thing rather than a collection of screens. It also made designing 50 plus screens genuinely faster and gave the development team a clear, scalable foundation.

Typography

Nunito Sans
Aa Bb Cc 123

Rounded and approachable at every size. It holds up well in dense data environments without feeling cold, which matters a lot on screens that are showing 200 rows of lead data.

Color System

#6840ED
#F1A027
#1D252C
#566F80
#F6F6F7

Purple for all primary actions and interactive elements. Amber for alerts and highlights. A dark to light neutral scale for everything else so color always carries meaning.

Component Library

KPI cards with trend indicators
Property cards with status chips
Lead stage badges in color
Sortable data tables
Multi step deal creation form
Activity log timeline
Modal dialogs and action menus

Spacing and Grid

8px base grid throughout. Cards carry 24px internal padding. Section rhythm at 40px vertical spacing. The sidebar sits at 64px collapsed so content always has the maximum possible room to breathe.

8 / 16 / 24 / 32 / 40px scale

Phase 6 — Lo Fidelity Wireframes

Structure First, Style Later

I worked through the key screens in lo fidelity before touching any colours or components. This is where the real structural thinking happened. Not what it looks like, but what goes where, what size, and in what order. Getting this right in grayscale meant the high fidelity phase moved much faster with far fewer painful revisions.

The questions I was answering at this stage were simple but important. Where does the eye land first? How many actions does a user need to complete the most common task? Does this layout still make sense when there are 200 records in a table instead of 10?

Dashboard. KPI strip always at the top so the admin reads the numbers before anything else. Charts fill the middle. Table data sits below for when you need to drill deeper.
Properties. Card grid over table. Each card shows photo, status chip, and lead count without opening anything. My Properties and All Properties tabs enforce the role separation.
Lead Profile. All context in one header row: contact, property interest, source, and assigned agent. Tabs below for matches, shortlisted, activity log, and discussion. No cross screen checking needed.
Deal Creation. Three step progress bar so users always know where they are. Deal amount shown prominently at the top. Each step is focused on one thing only, no overwhelming long form scroll.
Agent Profile. Agent info and four key KPIs in one horizontal band at the top. Three performance charts below for monthly trends, deal distribution, and conversion rate. All deals listed at the bottom. One page, no navigation needed.
The change that came out of wireframing

In my first dashboard wireframe the leads table was the first thing you saw when you logged in. After reviewing it with stakeholders, we moved the KPI cards above the table so admins could read the business health summary before diving into individual records. It sounds like a small thing but it changed the entire feel of opening the product each morning.

Phase 7 — UI Design

The Screens and the Thinking Behind Them

With the structure validated, I moved into high fidelity. What follows are the key screens from the final design, each with the specific thinking behind the decisions I made. These are not just polished mockups. Every choice was made to solve a specific problem identified in discovery.

1
Admin Dashboard
Why it works: Four KPI cards sit at the very top so Omar reads the numbers the moment the page loads. Total Leads, Deals Initiated, Deals Closed, and Average Deal Value are the four things that matter most to him every morning. Below that, listing performance by channel breaks down how properties are distributed across Property Finder, Bayut, Dubizzle, and other sources. Charts replace raw data tables wherever the goal is understanding trends rather than finding a specific record.
2
Properties Grid
Why it works: Property cards replace the old table. Each one shows a real photo, price, location, status chip, and active lead count so you can assess a listing at a glance without clicking into it. The My Properties and All Properties tab split keeps the role based separation working within a shared module, so agents see their own listings by default while admins can see everything.
3
Property Details
Why it works: Full photo gallery, pricing, location map, and all property specs are visible on a single page without navigating anywhere else. The three tabs below handle Property Overview, Activity Log, and Discussion Forum so related context stays organised without adding navigation depth. Action buttons stay fixed in the top bar so they are always reachable no matter how far you have scrolled.
4
Active Leads on a Property
Why it works: Every lead on a property shows their current stage as a coloured chip: Connected, Viewing, Negotiation. You can scan the whole table without reading a single word. Source platform logos from Property Finder, Bayut, and Dubizzle appear alongside each lead so you know where they came from without opening the record.
5
Lead Profile
Why it works: This was one of the biggest improvements in the whole redesign. Contact info, property interest, budget range, lead source, and assigned agent all live in one structured header. No more opening three screens to understand a single lead. The tabs below surface property matches with percentage scores, shortlisted properties, a full activity log, and a discussion forum. The most important actions, changing stage, editing info, and transferring the lead, stay visible at the top at all times.
6
Deal Review for Admin
Why it works: The purple header banner immediately shows the deal amount and total receipts including VAT. Those are the two numbers an admin needs to see first. The status chip tells you exactly what action is required right now. Commission details, split structure, and all the supporting fields are organised below in clear sections. Approve, Reject, and Request Change sit fixed at the top so you never have to scroll to take action.
7
Deal View for Agent
Why it works: This is the same deal that the admin sees, but filtered for what Sara actually needs. Her commission split, the key parties involved, and the supporting documents. Buyer details are hidden from the seller agent and vice versa, exactly as specified in the BRD. She sees only the actions available to her. This is the role based design working as intended on a single screen and without any duplication in the codebase.
8
Agent Performance Profile
Why it works: This page did not exist before this redesign. Admins had no way to evaluate an individual agent without exporting data to a spreadsheet. Now they get four headline metrics at the top, Total Deals Closed, Average Deal Value, Commission Earned, and Conversion Rate, followed by monthly performance trends, deal distribution by outcome, and a comparison of assigned properties versus deals actually closed. Charts throughout because a trend line communicates so much more than a number sitting in a table.
Outcome

What Was Actually Delivered

After three to four weeks of design work, I handed off a complete dev ready Figma file to the development team. The product is currently being built. Here is what went into that file.

50+
High fidelity screens designed and handed off
6
Core modules completely rebuilt from scratch
2
Distinct role based experiences in one product
1
Scalable design system for the dev team to build on
✅

Clearer for Admins

A KPI first dashboard, unified lead profiles, and visual deal tracking mean Omar can read the state of his business in under a minute instead of half a morning.

🎯

Focused for Agents

Sara now sees only what belongs to her work. Her leads, her listings, her deals, and her commission. Nothing else competes for her attention.

🚀

Ready to Sell

The product now looks and feels like something worth paying for. The business can demo it confidently and present it to enterprise clients without apology.

Reflection

What This Project Taught Me

01

Role based thinking changes everything in B2B

The single biggest improvement in this project came not from any visual decision but from the structural one of separating the admin and agent experiences. When each person only sees what belongs to their role, the platform feels simpler, faster, and less overwhelming without removing a single feature.

02

Getting the structure right before touching the visuals pays back immediately

Time I spent in IA and wireframes came back with interest during the high fidelity phase. Because I already knew what went where and why, every visual decision was faster and more confident. Skipping this step is the thing that causes most design projects to require painful rework later.

03

A product can serve its users and sell itself at the same time

This project taught me that operational clarity and demo quality are not in tension with each other. A well structured, logically organised interface is an impressive interface. You do not need to sacrifice usability to look polished. The two goals reinforce each other when the design is done properly.

04

What I would do differently with more time

This project moved quickly and relied on stakeholder input rather than direct user research. If I had more time I would run usability sessions on the deal submission flow and the lead management workflow with real admins and agents before finalising anything. Some decisions I made confidently based on the BRD and competitive research would have been even sharper with actual user feedback sitting behind them.

Thanks for reading

If you have thoughts on this case study or want to talk about working together, I would genuinely love to hear from you.
